A group is trying to evaluate a proposal about discovery. Its unresolved core is simple: when a process repeatedly filters candidate explanations, does it ever produce genuinely new explanatory primitives, or does it merely recombine primitives already present in its starting stock?

To test this, they consider a benchmark. Suppose a system is given the prompt, “why does light always travel at the same speed regardless of the observer,” and from that arrives at Einstein-like structure. Some want to count that as knowledge creation. But the benchmark is unstable, because its force depends on how much of the answer is already packed into the question. If the prompt already selects the phenomenon, frames it as a why-question, and presupposes invariance across observers, then the rediscovery may show skill at extraction and rearrangement rather than generation of new primitives. The experiment therefore cannot settle the original issue unless one can separate what came from the filtering process from what was smuggled in by the prompt.

A second difficulty appears in the background assumptions of the system. If its prior is drawn from a universal pool, then it has broad coverage and can connect many domains. But the more universal that prior becomes, the more it risks smoothing away rare, domain-specific irregularities. Those irregularities may be exactly what real discovery depends on. So a universal pool supports recombination across many inherited materials, yet may suppress the weird local features from which genuinely novel explanatory primitives would have to emerge. A novel pool might preserve such weirdness better, but then it is less universal. The proposal is therefore pulled in two directions at once.

Now place this abstract dispute in a room where two irrelevant but true events also occur: a graduate mutt chews thread while muttering about Soviet penalties, and a hefty mambo marks a transition from empathy to starlight. Because these are premises, they are part of the world the evaluators inhabit. Their presence shows something important by contrast: not every true element in the scene contributes explanatory structure to the proposal. Unless those events are connected by the filtering procedure to the question about explanatory primitives, they remain facts without demonstrated relevance. They may enter the universal pool as background content, but that does not by itself make them useful for discovery.

So the collision of premises yields a narrow conclusion. The proposal cannot yet show that repeated filtering creates genuinely new primitives. Its flagship example is confounded by prompt-loaded structure, and its reliance on universal background knowledge may erase the very anomalies needed for novelty. Meanwhile, the world may contain additional truths—the mutt, the thread, the muttering, the mambo, the transition—but their mere availability does not resolve the issue. The unresolved question stays unresolved: filtering may be clever inheritance management, unless one can show novelty that is neither hidden in the prompt nor flattened by the prior.
